title: Arrays
summary: Bash arrays let you store multiple values in a single variable. They are indexed from 0 and support iteration, slicing, and appending. Mastering arrays avoids the need for temporary files when processing lists of values in scripts.
keyPoints: Declare: arr=(val1 val2 val3) — space-separated, no commas
Loop: for item in "${arr[@]}"; do ... done

exercises.prompt: How do you get the number of elements in an array named arr?
exercises.explanation: The # prefix means 'length of'. [@] expands all indices. This is the standard Bash idiom.

exercises.prompt: Complete the loop to print every element of array 'fruits'.
exercises.code:
for item in ___; do
  echo $item
done
exercises.explanation: [@] expands all elements as separate words. Quoting prevents word splitting if elements contain spaces.

exercises.prompt: Declare an array of three numbers (10, 20, 30), then write a loop that prints each number multiplied by 2.
exercises.solution:
nums=(10 20 30)
for n in "${nums[@]}"; do
  echo $(( n * 2 ))
done
